Title: He made RM8,000 from recycling — and paid for surgery
Article:
NILAI: A 74-year-old retiree has turned his recycling efforts into a source of funding for eye surgery, after collecting thousands of ringgit through a community initiative.
Jaafar Idrus said he has redeemed about RM8,000 in reward points through the KitaRecycle programme under SWM Environment since taking it up in 2021.
The former Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI) employee said he began recycling to stay active in retirement, but the effort later proved useful when he developed cataracts in his left eye about two years ago.
"My vision gradually became more blurred and it was dangerous to drive, especially at night. After a check-up, the doctor confirmed I had cataracts and advised me to undergo surgery," he said.
Jaafar underwent the procedure at Hospital Universiti Putra Malaysia on Jan 9, paying RM1,300 for a replacement lens using funds redeemed from his recycling points.
"The surgery itself did not cost thousands of ringgit, but I'm grateful I could use the money from recycling to cover the lens without touching my savings," he said.
He added that his interest in recycling began when the programme was introduced in his neighbourhood in Taman Mulia Lagenda.
In addition to collecting recyclable items from his own home, Jaafar said neighbours often call him to pick up unwanted materials.
"I also collect items like cardboard, plastic bottles and cans that are still in good condition if I find them outside shops or by the roadside before bringing them home," he said.
He has also encouraged his children and grandchildren to adopt recycling habits, saying environmental awareness should be nurtured from a young age.
Jaafar, who has won several recycling competitions at various levels, urged the public to take an interest in separating waste and practising recycling.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang water supply remains stable despite heatwave alert
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Penang's water supply remains robust despite the ongoing heatwave, with river and dam levels comfortably above critical thresholds, according to the Penang Water Supply Corporation (PBAPP).
PBAPP chief executive officer Datuk K. Pathmanathan said there was no immediate cause for concern as current raw water levels, particularly at Sungai Muda, remained stable.
"As of Sunday, the water level at Sungai Muda's Lahar Tiang intake is 2.31 metres, which is well above the Level 1 alert threshold of two metres.
"There are no issues at the moment," he told the New Straits Times.
Pathmanathan said this indicated that the heatwave had yet to significantly affect Penang's primary raw water source, which supplies more than 80 per cent of the state's needs.
He said all three key dams in Penang were also at healthy levels, reinforcing supply security.
"Air Itam Dam is at 73.4 per cent, Teluk Bahang Dam at 86.0 per cent, and Mengkuang Dam at 91.4 per cent.
"Overall, dam levels are good," he added.
On the impact of prolonged dry weather, Pathmanathan said Penang could sustain its water supply for extended periods under controlled drawdown strategies.
He said Air Itam Dam could last up to 72 days at a controlled drawdown rate of 22 million litres per day (MLD), while Teluk Bahang Dam could last 261 days at 60 MLD.
Mengkuang Dam, he added, has sufficient reserves for 132 days at 600 MLD.
Pathmanathan said the situation remained well within manageable limits.
Pathmanathan also highlighted that PBAPP's preparedness was the result of long-term planning and proactive measures.
"This is based on three years of planning, from the Air Itam Dam Action Plan followed by the Double Dam Action Plan," he said.
On concerns that similar heatwave conditions in Kedah could affect Sungai Muda, a critical raw water source for Penang, he said the river level remained safely above the alert threshold.
"The water level at Sungai Muda is still above Level 1 alert, so the situation is good at the moment," he said.
Yesterday, the Malaysian Meteorological Department issued a Level 1 (alert) heatwave warning for several areas, including the northeast and Seberang Prai Utara districts in Penang.
It said 15 other areas in Peninsular Malaysia were also experiencing similar conditions, including the whole of Perlis and Kuala Lumpur, as well as Sik, Pokok Sena, Kubang Pasu, Langkawi, Kuala Muda, Kulim and Bandar Baharu in Kedah.
A similar weather warning has also been issued for Hulu Perak, Selama, Kinta and Kuala Kangsar in Perak.
A
heatwave warning
is issued when daily maximum temperatures range between 37 and 40 degrees Celsius for at least three consecutive days, while the alert level applies to temperatures between 35 and 37 degrees Celsius over the same period.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel's David's Sling system failed to intercept Iranian missiles
Article:
TEL AVIV: A malfunction in the David's Sling aerial interceptor system allowed two Iranian ballistic missiles to strike southern Israel, wounding dozens of people over the weekend, the military confirmed Monday.
The towns of Dimona and Arad were hit on Saturday evening, with the former widely believed to hold Israel's undeclared nuclear arsenal.
Most of the wounded were injured by shrapnel or while rushing to shelters, Israeli first responders said.
Several buildings were also damaged in the strikes.
Israeli financial daily and website Calcalist first reported that it was David's Sling, a key component of Israel's multi-layered air defence shield, that had failed to intercept the incoming Iranian ballistic missiles.
Contacted by AFP, the Israeli military confirmed that it was David's Sling, named after the Biblical figure who slew Goliath, that had malfunctioned.
Operational since April 2017, the aerial defence system was originally designed to counter heavy rockets and cruise missiles.
Long-range ballistic missiles of the kind fired by Iran are typically handled by the Arrow system, which intercepts targets outside the atmosphere, while shorter-range threats fall to the Iron Dome and the newly deployed Iron Beam laser system.
Calcalist reported that the military chose to use David's Sling on Saturday following a recent upgrade in a bid to preserve Arrow interceptor stocks amid concerns the war with Iran could drag on.
On Feb 11, the defence ministry announced that David's Sling had successfully completed a series of tests as part of what it described as a "future threat readiness upgrade."
The war with Iran began on Feb 28.
Yoav Tourgeman, chief executive of Rafael, the system's main contractor, was quoted as saying that the upgraded version "incorporated new capabilities across a wide range of challenging scenarios."
Despite Saturday's failed interceptions, the military has stressed that its forces have intercepted about 92 per cent of the more than 400 ballistic missiles fired by Iran since the start of the war.
Military spokesman Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani said on Sunday that there had been only four direct hits on Israeli territory since the Iran war began.
The Middle East was sparked by joint US-Israeli strikes on Iran that triggered a wave of retaliatory missile and drone attacks against Israel and several other countries in the region.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kim Teck Cheong appoints former chief secretary Mohd Zuki Ali as chairman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kim Teck Cheong Consolidated Bhd (KTC) has named Tan Sri Mohd Zuki Ali, former government chief secretary, as chairman of the Board, a move aimed at strengthening governance and supporting the company's long-term growth strategy.
Mohd Zuki will serve as non-executive chairman, providing oversight and strategic guidance to the Board in line with the company's long-term priorities.
In a statement, KTC said that Mohd Zuki brings decades of public service and leadership experience, along with deep institutional insight.
His appointment is expected to further enhance KTC's strategic direction and engagement with stakeholders across both public and private sectors.
Datuk Dexter Lau, KTC executive director, said: "We are honoured to welcome Tan Sri Zuki to KTC. His extensive leadership experience and understanding of national priorities will provide valuable guidance as we continue to strengthen our governance and build a resilient, forward-looking organisation."
"As we expand our operations, we are anchoring national and regional expansion from East Malaysia as a strategic growth hub, while continuing to work closely with partners across the industry to enhance supply chain connectivity, alongside advancing strategic expansion and investment initiatives across Malaysia and the wider region," Lau said.
He added that KTC remains committed to supporting supply chain efficiency and improving market access, particularly across East Malaysia, while building a strong foundation for sustainable growth at both national and regional levels.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelington bags RM413.7mil semiconductor contract in India
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kelington Group Bhd's unit, Kelington Engineering (S) Pte Ltd, has bagged a US$105 million (about RM413.7 million) contract to deliver a turnkey gas distribution system for a semiconductor wafer fabrication facility in Gujarat, India.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, the group said the contract was awarded by a leading semiconductor manufacturer in India and involves the design, supply and installation of a mission-critical gas distribution system.
Kelington said such systems are essential to ensure a continuous and highly controlled supply of ultra-high purity gases, supporting precision, yield and operational reliability in semiconductor fabrication processes.
Work on the project has commenced and is scheduled for completion by July 2028.
The group expects the project to contribute positively to its earnings and net assets for the financial years ending Dec 31, 2026 until completion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pakistan ranked most polluted country in 2025, data shows
Article:
SINGAPORE: Pakistan was ranked the world's smoggiest country in 2025, with concentrations of hazardous small particles known as PM2.5 up to 13 times higher than the recommended World Health Organisation level, research showed on Tuesday.
Swiss air quality monitoring firm IQAir said in its annual report that 13 countries and territories kept average PM2.5 levels at the WHO standard of less than 5 microgrammes per cubic metre last year, up from seven in 2024.
Following are some of the findings of the report:
* In total, 130 out of 143 monitored countries and territories failed to meet the WHO guideline.
* Bangladesh and Tajikistan were second and third on the most polluted list.
* Chad, statistically the smoggiest country of 2024, ranked fourth in 2025, but the decline in PM2.5 concentrations last year is likely to be the result of data gaps.
* Last March, the United States shut down a global monitoring programme that compiled pollution data collected from its embassy and consulate buildings, citing budget constraints.
* "The loss of the data in March made it appear there was a significant drop in PM2.5 levels (in Chad), but the fact of the matter is that we don't know," said Christi Chester Schroeder, lead author of the IQAir report.
* The US decision eliminated a primary data source for many smog-prone countries, and Burundi, Turkmenistan and Togo were excluded from the 2025 report because of information gaps.
* India's Loni was the world's most polluted city in 2025, with average PM2.5 levels of 112.5 micrograms, followed by Hotan in the northwestern Chinese region of Xinjiang at 109.6 micrograms.
* The world's top 25 most polluted cities were all in India, Pakistan and China.
* Only 14 per cent of the world's cities met the WHO standard in 2025, down from 17 per cent a year earlier, with Canadian wildfires driving up PM2.5 across the United States and as far as Europe.
* Among the countries that met the standard in 2025 were Australia, Iceland, Estonia and Panama.
* Laos, Cambodia and Indonesia all reported significant PM2.5 reductions compared to the previous year, thanks mainly to wetter and windier La Nina weather.
Mongolia saw average concentrations fall 31 per cent to 17.8 micrograms per cubic metre.
* In all, 75 countries reported lower PM2.5 levels in 2025 compared to a year earlier, with 54 recording higher average concentrations, IQAir said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loose cattle spark spate of road accidents in Gua Musang
Article:
GUA MUSANG: Free-roaming livestock on several main roads in this district have been described as a "death trap" for motorists, particularly during the festive travel period.
Police say five accidents involving vehicles hitting stray cattle have been recorded between March 19 and today, coinciding with the start of increased holiday traffic.
Gua Musang district police chief Superintendent Sik Choon Foo said the incidents occurred over six days as travellers returned to their hometowns for Aidilfitri.
Although none of the crashes were fatal, he said motorists had suffered vehicle damage and financial losses.
"This issue requires cooperation from all parties, especially livestock owners, who must take responsibility for their animals. Such incidents could also result in fatal accidents, particularly for motorcyclists," he said when contacted.
Despite repeated warnings, the problem persists, he added, urging farmers to keep their livestock confined to prevent them from straying onto roads during the busy festive season.
"The five cases in six days should be taken seriously, as they affect road users who are travelling home to celebrate Aidilfitri with their families," he said.
One motorist, Mohd Saiful Razali, 34, said he and his family narrowly avoided a collision with cattle on the road.
He said the incident occurred while travelling from Gua Musang to Gerik, Perak, along the Gua Musang–Jeli route.
"When we reached the Kesedar Meranto settlement area, several cows were wandering on the road, and I nearly hit them," he said.
"We were fortunate to be travelling during the day and managed to brake in time. It was a dangerous situation, and sudden braking could also cause vehicles behind us to crash."
He called on livestock owners to better control their animals, particularly during the holiday period when traffic is heavier.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 25-year-old caught driving uninsured brother's car without licence
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 25-year-old man driving his brother's uninsured car without a licence was among those detained during a Road Transport Department (RTD) operation on the Kuala Lumpur-Karak Expressway yesterday.
The man was heading back to the federal capital from Kelantan when he was stopped by Kuala Lumpur RTD officers.
Kuala Lumpur RTD director Hamidi Adam said the driver claimed he had borrowed the vehicle from his brother to return to his hometown for the Hari Raya holidays.
"Further inspections revealed that he did not have a valid driving licence. In addition, the car's Motor Vehicle Licence had expired a year ago and it had no insurance coverage," he said in a statement today.
Hamidi said the vehicle was seized under the Road Transport Act 1987.
"Kuala Lumpur RTD consistently monitors all complaints and carries out continuous enforcement to ensure all vehicles comply with established road rules and laws," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Immigration defends Hari Raya enforcement after ex-minister's criticism
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Immigration Department has defended its enforcement actions during the Aidilfitri period following criticism from a former law minister over the timing of operations against undocumented migrants.
Immigration deputy director-general (operations) Datuk Lokman Effendi Ramli said enforcement was carried out to maintain public order and was neither seasonal nor selective.
"Operations are implemented continuously and professionally in accordance with the law," he said when contacted.
Lokman said this approach reflects the department's commitment to consistent enforcement.
He added that while the department respected the need for everyone to celebrate their respective festivities, the law must be obeyed by all, including foreigners.
"When in Rome, do as the Romans do. I want to stress that no action will be taken against foreigners who possess valid documents and comply with immigration laws.
"Enforcement is only focused on those who violate regulations," he said.
Yesterday, former minister in the Prime Minister's Department (law) Datuk Zaid Ibrahim criticised the department over the arrests of undocumented migrants, questioning the sensitivity of such operations.
"If enforcement is relaxed, there is a risk that certain parties will take advantage to break the law, including activities involving undocumented migrants," Lokman said.
He added that the department would continue to carry out operations to ensure the rule of law is preserved.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man seen slapping shopkeeper in viral video
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A video showing a man slapping a shopkeeper has gone viral on social media, although the exact location of the incident has not been established.
The
video
shows the man arguing with the shopkeeper before slapping him as other customers inside the grocery shop look on.
The incident is believed to have started inside the shop and continued outside, where the man was restrained by a group of people.
CCTV footage
shows the man at the counter acting aggressively towards a female employee before a struggle breaks out. Several people step in to restrain him.
Items on the counter were scattered as the situation escalated.
Another clip shows the same man being restrained by several individuals outside the premises before a nearby security guard intervenes.
Based on the timestamp on the CCTV footage, the incident is believed to have occurred at 8.20am on March 20.
However, the cause of the incident has not been confirmed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops nab fake 'immigration' officer who robbed foreigners
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested a 32-year-old man who allegedly posed as an immigration officer to rob foreigners.
Dang Wangi police chief Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam said the suspect was detained at 1.30pm yesterday following a tip-off.
Sazalee said investigations revealed the suspect would approach foreign men and identify himself as an immigration officer before demanding their passports.
"The suspect would then flee with the victims' wallets and cash," he said in a statement.
"The arrest has helped solve two robbery cases reported earlier. Further investigations are underway to complete the probe before the matter is referred to the deputy public prosecutor for further action."
Police also seized clothing, a motorcycle, a helmet, and a food delivery bag believed to have been used during the crimes.
Those with information are urged to contact the Dang Wangi district police headquarters at 03-26002222 or the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump approved Iran operation after Netanyahu argued for joint killing of Khamenei
Article:
WASHINGTON: Less than 48 hours before the US-Israeli strike on Iran began, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu spoke by phone to President Donald Trump about the reasons for launching the kind of complex, far-off war the American leader once had campaigned against.
Both Trump and Netanyahu knew from intelligence briefings earlier in the week that Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his key lieutenants would soon meet at his compound in Teheran, making them vulnerable to a "decapitation strike" – an attack against a country's top leaders often used by Israelis but traditionally less so by the United States.
But new intelligence suggested that the meeting had been moved forward to Saturday morning from Saturday night, according to three people briefed on the call.
The call has not been previously reported.
Netanyahu, determined to move forward with an operation he had urged for decades, argued that there might never be a better chance to kill Khamenei and to avenge previous Iranian efforts to assassinate Trump, these people said.
Those included a murder-for-hire plot allegedly orchestrated by Iran in 2024, when Trump was a candidate.
The Justice ‌Department has accused a Pakistani man of trying to recruit people in the United States in the plan, meant as retaliation for Washington's killing of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard ​Corps' top commander, Qassem Soleimani.
By the time the call took place, Trump already had approved the idea of the United States carrying out a military operation against Iran but had not yet decided when or under what circumstances the United States would get involved, said the sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive internal deliberations.
The US military had for weeks built up a presence in the region, prompting many within the administration to conclude it was just a matter of when the president would decide to move forward. One possible date, just a few days earlier, had been scuttled because of bad weather.
Reuters was unable to determine how Netanyahu's argument affected Trump as he contemplated issuing orders to strike, but the call amounted to the Israeli leader's closing argument to his U.S. counterpart.
The three sources briefed on the call said they believed it - along with the intelligence showing a closing window to kill Iran's leader - was a catalyst for Trump's final decision to order the military on Feb 27 to move ahead with Operation Epic Fury.
Trump could make history by helping eliminate an Iranian leadership long reviled by the West and by many Iranians, Netanyahu argued. Iranians might even take to the streets, he said, overthrowing a theocratic system that had governed the country since 1979 and been a leading source of global terrorism and instability ever since.
The first bombs struck on Saturday morning, February 28. Trump announced that evening that Khamenei was dead.
In response to a request for comment, White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly did not directly address the call between Trump and Netanyahu but told Reuters the military operation was designed to "destroy the Iranian regime's ballistic missile and production capacity, annihilate the Iranian regime's Navy, end their ability to arm proxies, and guarantee that Iran can never obtain a nuclear weapon."
Neither Netanyahu's office nor Iran's UN representative responded to comment requests.
Netanyahu in a news conference on Thursday dismissed as "fake news" claims that "Israel somehow dragged the US into a conflict with Iran.
Does anyone really think that someone can tell President Trump what to do? Come on."
Trump has said publicly that the decision to strike was his alone.
Reuters reporting, with officials and others close to both leaders speaking mostly on condition of anonymity given the sensitivity of internal deliberations, does not suggest that Netanyahu forced Trump to go to war.
But the reporting shows that the Israeli leader was an effective advocate and that his framing of the decision – including the opportunity to kill an Iranian leader who allegedly had overseen efforts to kill Trump – was persuasive to the president.
Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth in early March suggested that revenge was at least one motive for the operation, telling reporters, "Iran tried to kill President Trump, and President Trump got the last laugh."
June attack targeted nuclear, missile sites
Trump ran his campaign in 2024 based on his first administration's foreign policy of "America First" and said publicly that he wanted to avoid war with Iran, preferring to deal with Teheran diplomatically.
But as discussions over Iran's nuclear program failed to produce a deal last spring, Trump began contemplating a strike, according to the three people familiar with White House deliberations.
A first attack came in June, when Israel bombed Iran's nuclear facilities and missile sites, and killed several Iranian leaders.
US forces later joined the attack, and when that joint operation ended after 12 days, Trump publicly reveled in the success, saying the US had "obliterated" Iran's nuclear facilities.
Yet months later, talks began again between the U.S. and Israel about a second aerial attack aimed at hitting additional missile facilities and preventing Iran from gaining the ability to build a nuclear weapon.
The Israelis also wanted to kill Khamenei, a longtime, bitter geopolitical foe who had repeatedly fired missiles into Israel and supported heavily armed proxy forces encircling the nation.
That included the Hamas militant group that launched the surprise attack on October 7, 2023, from Gaza, and Hizbollah, based in Lebanon.
The Israelis began to plan their attack on Iran under the assumption they would be acting alone, Defense Minister Israel Katz told Israel's N12 News on March 5.
But during a December visit to Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida, Netanyahu told Trump that he was not fully satisfied with the outcome of the joint operation in June, said two people familiar with the relationship between the two leaders, speaking on condition of anonymity.
Trump indicated he was open to another bombing campaign, the people added, but he also wanted to try another round of diplomatic talks.
Two events pushed Trump toward attacking Iran again, according to several US and Israeli officials and diplomats.
The US operation on Jan 3 to capture Venezuela's President Nicolas Maduro in Caracas - which resulted in no American deaths while removing from power a longstanding US foe - demonstrated the possibility that ambitious military operations could have few collateral consequences for US forces.
Later that same month, massive anti-government protests erupted in Iran, prompting a vicious response by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, killing thousands. Trump vowed to help the protesters but did little immediately that was public.
Privately, however, cooperation intensified between the Israel Defense Forces and the US military's Middle East command, known as CENTCOM, with joint military planning conducted during secret meetings, according to two Israeli officials, speaking on condition of anonymity.
Not long after, during a February visit by Netanyahu to Washington, the Israeli leader briefed Trump on Iran's growing ballistic missile program, pointing out specific sites of concern.
He also laid out the dangers of the ballistic missile program, including the risk that Iran might eventually gain the ability to strike the American homeland, said three people familiar with the private conversations.
The White House did not respond to questions about Trump's December and February meetings with Netanyahu.
Trump's chance at history
By late February, many U.S. officials and regional diplomats considered a US attack on Iran very likely to proceed, though the details remained uncertain, according to two other US officials, one Israeli official and two additional officials familiar with the matter.
Trump was briefed by Pentagon and intelligence officials on the potential advantages to be gained from a successful attack, including the decimation of Iran's missile program, according to two people familiar with those briefings.
Before the phone call between Netanyahu and Trump, Secretary of State Marco Rubio told a small group of top Congressional leaders on Feb 24 that Israel was likely to attack Iran, whether or not the US participated, and Iran would then likely retaliate against US targets, according to three people briefed on the meeting.
Behind Rubio's warning was an assessment by American intelligence officials that such an attack would indeed provoke counterstrikes from Iran against US diplomatic and military outposts and US Gulf allies, said three sources familiar with US intelligence reports.
This prediction proved accurate. The strikes have led to Iranian counterattacks on US military assets, the deaths of more than 2,300 Iranian civilians and at least 13 U.S. service members, attacks on US.
Gulf allies, the closure of one of the world's most vital shipping routes and a historic spike in oil prices that is already being felt by consumers in the United States and beyond.
Trump had also been briefed that there was a chance, even if small, that the killing of Iran's top leaders could usher in a government in Teheran that was more willing to negotiate with Washington, said two other people familiar with Rubio's briefing.
The possibility of regime change was one of Netanyahu's arguments in the call shortly before Trump gave final orders to attack Iran, said the people briefed on it.
That view was not held by the Central Intelligence Agency, which had assessed in the weeks prior that Khamenei would likely be replaced by an internal hardliner if he was killed, as Reuters previously reported.
The CIA did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Trump repeatedly called for an uprising after Khamenei was killed. With the war in its fourth week and the region engulfed in conflict, Iran's Revolutionary Guards still patrol the nation's streets. Millions of Iranians remain sheltered in their homes.
Khamenei's son Mojtaba, considered even more harshly anti-American than his father, has been named the new supreme leader of Iran.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man beaten after allegedly trying to steal motorcycle in Ipoh
Article:
IPOH: A man was allegedly beaten by a group of individuals in Station 18 here after he was suspected of trying to steal a motorcycle.
The incident was captured in a video showing the man being surrounded, forced to the ground and assaulted by several individuals.
Several bystanders and motorcycles could also be seen.
The video was shared on X by user @update11111, who claimed the incident involved a group of motorcyclists.
However, the circumstances leading up to the incident have not been independently verified.
It is not known whether a police report has been lodged.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Measat expands in South Asia with new Nepali-language channel
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Measat Global Bhd has strengthened its footprint in South Asia with the launch of a new Nepali-language channel broadcast via its Measat-3d satellite.
The company is providing satellite broadcasting services for Angel TV Namaskar, which is part of the Angel TV Networks group, aimed primarily at Nepali audiences.
The partnership leverages Measat's satellite coverage to deliver high-definition content across the region as well as enhancing the network's international distribution aspirations.
Measat chief commercial officer Ganendra Selvaraj said the collaboration underscores the company's position as a leading satellite solutions provider in South Asia.
"The addition of Angel TV Namaskar further diversifies the growing range of channels within our video neighbourhood, reinforcing the continued relevance of satellite broadcasting in an evolving content landscape," he added.
Angel TV Networks chief operations officer Subash Raja added that the use of Measat-3d ensures a high-quality viewing experience tailored to Nepal's cultural context.
"There is significant demand for lifestyle and entertainment content in the region, and we are pleased to contribute a new supply of constructive, family-friendly content for viewers," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Airlines cancel more flights as Middle East conflict escalates
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Global air travel remains severely disrupted after the war in Iran forced the closure of major Middle Eastern hubs, including Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi, stranding tens of thousands of passengers.
Below is the latest on flights, in alphabetical order:
AEGEAN AIRLINES
Greece's largest carrier has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv, Beirut and Amman until April 22 and to Erbil and Baghdad until May 24. Flights to Dubai have been cancelled until April 19 and to Riyadh until April 18.
AIRBALTIC
Latvia's airBaltic said all flights to Tel Aviv had been cancelled until April 29. All flights to Dubai stand cancelled until October 24.
AIR CANADA
The Canadian carrier has cancelled all flights to Tel Aviv until May 2 and to Dubai until March 28.
AIR EUROPA
The Spanish airline has cancelled all flights to Tel Aviv until April 10.
AIR FRANCE KLM
Air France has cancelled Tel Aviv and Beirut flights until March 28 and Dubai and Riyadh flights until March 24, including the March 25 departure from Dubai.
KLM suspended flights to Riyadh, Dammam and Dubai until May 17 and to Tel Aviv until April 11.
CATHAY PACIFIC
The Hong Kong airline has cancelled all passenger and cargo flights to Dubai and Riyadh until April 30. It has, however, added extra flights to London between March 21 and March 28 due to increased demand.
DELTA
The US carrier has cancelled its New York-Tel Aviv flights until May 31 and Tel Aviv-New York flights until June 1. The restart of its Atlanta-Tel Aviv route has been delayed, with flights to Tel Aviv paused until August 4 and from Tel Aviv until August 5.
EL AL ISRAEL AIRLINES
The Israeli carrier said operational constraints are preventing regular flights from Israel, except in rare, exceptional cases, and that it is continuing efforts to bring passengers home. The airline has also urged authorities to open Ramon Airport near Eilat.
EMIRATES
The UAE airline said it was operating a reduced flight schedule following a partial reopening of regional airspace.
ETIHAD AIRWAYS
The UAE carrier said it was operating a limited commercial flight schedule between Abu Dhabi and select destinations.
FINNAIR
The Finnish carrier has cancelled its Dubai flights until March 29 and Doha flights until July 2, while continuing to avoid the airspace of Iraq, Iran, Syria and Israel.
FLYNAS
Saudi budget airline Flynas has extended its suspension of flights to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Doha, Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq and Syria until March 31.
IAG
IAG-owned British Airways has extended cancellations of flights to Amman, Bahrain, Dubai and Tel Aviv until May 31 and to Doha until April 30, while adding flights to Bangkok and Singapore. Flights to Abu Dhabi remain suspended until later this year.
INDIGO
The Indian airline has suspended operations to Doha, Kuwait, Bahrain, Dammam, Fujairah, Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah until March 28.
JAPAN AIRLINES
Japan Airlines has suspended scheduled Tokyo-Doha flights until March 31 and Doha-Tokyo flights until April 1.
LOT
The Polish airline said all its flights to Dubai stand cancelled until March 28 and to Tel Aviv until May 31. It has also cancelled flights to Riyadh until April 30 and to Beirut from March 31 to April 30.
LUFTHANSA GROUP
The German airline group, which includes Lufthansa, Austrian Airlines, SWISS, Brussels Airlines and ITA Airways, has suspended flights to Tel Aviv through April 9 and to Beirut, Dubai, Amman, Erbil and Abu Dhabi until March 28. Flights to Tehran are suspended through April 30 and to Riyadh until April 5.
MALAYSIA AIRLINES
The Malaysian carrier has suspended all flights to Doha until March 28.
NORWEGIAN AIR
The low-cost airline has pushed back planned launches of its Tel Aviv and Beirut services to June 15, from April 1 and April 4, respectively. It has cancelled all Dubai flights through April 8.
PEGASUS
Turkey's Pegasus Airlines has cancelled its Iran, Iraq, Amman, Beirut, Kuwait, Bahrain, Doha, Dammam, Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Sharjah flights until April 13. Flights to Riyadh have been cancelled until March 24.
QATAR AIRWAYS
The carrier said it would operate a revised limited number of flights until March 28.
SINGAPORE AIRLINES
Singapore Airlines said it will extend the suspension of its Singapore-Dubai flights until April 30 and add services between Singapore and London Gatwick from March 31 to October 24 and on the Singapore-Melbourne route from March 29 to October 24 to meet higher demand.
TURKISH AIRLINES
Turkish Airlines has cancelled most Middle East flights until the end of March. SunExpress, its joint venture with Lufthansa, has cancelled flights to Dubai and Bahrain until March 23.
VIETNAM AIRLINES
Vietnam's flag carrier has planned to cancel 23 flights per week across several domestic routes from April.
WIZZ AIR
The low-cost airline has suspended flights to Israel until March 29 and to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Amman and Jeddah from mainland European destinations until mid-September.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Steven Sim's aide lodges police report over false claim on hospital relocation
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: An aide to Bukit Mertajam member of parliament Steven Sim Chee Keong has lodged a police report over "defamatory and provocative" social media claims regarding the alleged relocation of Bukit Mertajam Hospital.
Lim Zheng Han said the report was made today following a Facebook post containing elements of racial and religious incitement alongside inaccurate claims about the facility.
He said the post, uploaded on March 22 by a Facebook account identified as "Hamly Hadi", used a screenshot from a video of Sim's visit to the hospital on Sept 15 last year.
"The visit was intended to review basic facilities and assess upgrading needs.
"However, the post was accompanied by an unfounded caption claiming that 'Bukit Mertajam Hospital will be relocated to avoid disturbing an Indian temple — Steven Sim Chee Keong'," he said today.
Lim said the claim was false, misleading and made with malicious intent.
He clarified that there had been no proposal to relocate the hospital, adding that authorities were instead studying plans to construct an additional building at the existing site to accommodate rising patient numbers.
"Such slander not only tarnishes the image of Steven Sim, but also risks causing tension and undermining harmony in Malaysia's multiracial society," he added.
Lim said the act could constitute offences under several laws, including Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, Sections 499 and 500 of the Penal Code for defamation, and Section 505(b) of the Penal Code for statements likely to cause public alarm.
He urged authorities to carry out an immediate investigation and take firm action against the account owner.
"Strict action without compromise is necessary to ensure that the spread of defamatory and inflammatory content does not continue," he said, adding that public order in Malaysia's plural society must be safeguarded.
The Bukit Mertajam Hospital's Board of Visitors yesterday dismissed allegations that the hospital would be relocated to avoid disrupting a temple.
It said the Penang government had already allocated eight acres of land for the expansion plan, which is currently under review by the Health Ministry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Couple remanded over viral indecent act at Penang cemetery
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Police have obtained a three-day remand order against a couple allegedly caught on video
engaging in an indecent act
at a cemetery here.
The remand order, from today until Thursday, was granted when the couple were brought before the magistrate's court this morning.
Magistrate Nadratun Naim Mohd Saidi issued the order to facilitate police investigations into the case.
Earlier, the couple arrived in handcuffs under police escort.
This morning, state police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail confirmed that police have detained the couple.
Both were picked up in separate locations around George Town yesterday.
According to northeast district police chief Assistant Commissioner Abdul Rozak Muhammad, they admitted to committing the act.
Police received a report about 12.05pm yesterday from a 69-year-old local man regarding a video showing a man and a woman behaving inappropriately at a Chinese cemetery.
Initial investigations found that the incident took place on March 22 at the Batu Gantung Chinese cemetery in Air Itam here.
Rozak said the case is being investigated under Section 294(a) and Section 509 of the Penal Code, as well as Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998.
Police arrested the 37-year-old woman about 8.35pm yesterday at a flat near Jalan Padang Tembak here, while the 58-year-old man was arrested about 11.10pm in front of a building near Jalan Paya Terubong in Sungai Ara, Bayan Lepas.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vietnam Airlines plans domestic route cuts due to jet fuel shortage, regulator says
Article:
HANOI: Flag carrier Vietnam Airlines plans to cancel 23 flights per week across several domestic routes from April because of looming jet fuel shortages, Vietnam's aviation authority said.
The majority of Vietnam's jet fuel demand is met by imports, and the war in the Middle East is disrupting supply.
Vietnam Airlines, part of state-owned Vietnam Airlines Corp, is prioritising routes critical to national connectivity, trade, tourism, diplomacy and domestic travel, the CAAV said in a statement issued late on Monday.
From April 1, it will suspend seven domestic routes and cancel 23 flights a week to conserve fuel, including services from Hai Phong to Buon Ma Thuot, Cam Ranh, Phu Quoc and Can Tho, and from Ho Chi Minh City to Van Don, Rach Gia and Dien Bien, the statement added.
Vietnam Airlines did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Reuters previously reported that the CAAV had warned of potential flight reductions from April after China and Thailand halted jet fuel exports because of the war in Iran, raising the risk of shortages.
Vietnamese airlines are preparing to implement fuel surcharges on international routes from early April, the authority added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Solarvest's LSS 5+ win signals growth momentum
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Solarvest Holdings Bhd's first successful bid under the large-scale solar (LSS) 5+ programme in March underscores the company's continued execution momentum, despite a recent pullback in its share price.
According to RHB Research, the project also represents a steady step towards its RM2 billion order book target by financial year 2026 (FY26), according to RHB Research.
The contract, secured via its subsidiary Atlantic Blue Sdn Bhd, involves an engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning (EPCC) agreement with Jubli Pasifik Sdn Bhd to develop a 36 megawatt alternating current (MWac) solar photovoltaic plant in Padang Siding, Perlis.
The project carries a value of RM89.5 million and is targeted for commercial operation by Feb 26, 2028.
RHB highlighted Solarvest's solid earnings visibility, underpinned by an outstanding order book of RM1.5 billion as at the third quarter of FY26.
Of this, about 68 per cent comes from LSS projects, 19 per cent from the Corporate Green Power Programme (CGPP), and the remaining 13 per cent from commercial, industrial and residential segments.
The firm said the removal of a nine per cent value-added tax rebate is expected to have a negligible impact on less than RM400 million worth of projects, as cost pressures are mitigated by lower secured panel prices and favourable procurement of inverters and cables, as well as a stronger ringgit against the US dollar.
It also expects solar panel prices to ease after April, driven by softer raw material costs and reduced front-loading activities.
Key inputs such as silver and polysilicon have already seen notable price declines year-to-date, reinforcing the view that major Chinese suppliers are unlikely to default on supply agreements.
RHB Research said Solarvest is currently trading at about 23.8 times price-to-earnings, near its three-year mean, which it views as an attractive re-entry opportunity into the renewable energy sector.
The firm kept its "Buy" call on the stock with a target price of RM3.49, implying a 48 per cent upside.
However, it cautioned that risks include weaker-than-expected contract wins, changes in project costs, reliance on government renewable energy policies, competitive pressures and potential cost escalations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Yinson set for stable FPSO income, backed by US$19.5bil backlog
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Yinson Holdings Bhd is expected to maintain stable earnings from its floating production, storage and offloading (FPSO) charter operations, supported by a US$19.5 billion backlog that underpins its long-term earnings, RHB Research said.
Its analyst Lee Yun Leon said that while reported earnings may remain volatile due to foreign exchange and mark-to-market movements, underlying profitability is expected to strengthen further with full-year contributions from Agogo from financial year ending Jan 31, 2027 (FY27), onwards.
He said balance sheet risk continues to improve with rising non-recourse debt and adequate liquidity to support new project wins, although the timing of FPSO awards and corporate exercises remain key uncertainties.
"We remain constructive on Yinson's medium-term outlook, underpinned by a growing base of recurring FPSO charter income and a strong US$19.5 billion backlog providing long-term earnings visibility," he said in a note.
On the FY26 results, Lee said Yinson's core net profit of RM659 million met 96 per cent of RHB Research's consensus estimates.
Yinson posted FY26 revenue of RM5.4 billion, down 28 per cent year-on-year, primarily due to lower engineering, procurement, construction, installation and commissioning contributions following the start of charter periods for FPSO Maria Quitéria, FPSO Agogo and FPSO Atlanta.
The company declared a dividend per share of one sen, bringing total FY26 dividends to 5 sen.
RHB Research retained a "Buy" rating on Yinson, raising its target price to RM3.83 from RM3.69 while maintaining the earnings forecasts.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'No one above the law': Dzulkefly on ministry staff smoking at restaurant
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Reaffirming his stance against double standards, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad stressed that no one is above the law following a smoking violation by a ministry official at an eatery.
The officer was issued a compound notice for smoking at a gazetted premises where the act is strictly prohibited.
Commenting on
the incident
, Dr Dzulkefly posted on X: "No one is above the law, including our own MOH staff. We must lead by example if we want the public to respect the law. No double standards."
The statement follows a report that the Health Ministry issued a
compound notice
to the officer for violating the Control of Smoking Products for Public Health Act (Act 852).
Under Act 852, any individual found smoking in a prohibited area faces a fine of up to RM5,000 or imprisonment of up to two years upon conviction.
Public health advocates have lauded the move, noting that enforcing the law against enforcement officers themselves builds public trust.
Dzulkefly has been a staunch proponent of the smoking ban in eateries and has consistently pushed for stricter enforcement of the Control of Smoking Products for Public Health Act.
The ministry has reiterated that members of the public who witness similar violations, regardless of who the offender is, should report them via the MOH WhatsApp hotline.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan to release national oil reserves from Thursday
Article:
TOKYO: Japan will release another part of its strategic oil reserves from Thursday, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said Tuesday, after Tokyo began to tap stockpiles last week.
"In order to secure 'the necessary amount for the whole of Japan' ...we will release the state (oil) reserve from 26th March this week," she said in a post on X.
Last Monday, Tokyo started the release of 15 days' worth of private-sector petroleum reserves.
Takaichi previously announced that a month's worth of government stockpiles would also be released.
Japan depends on the Middle East for around 95 percent of its oil imports.
Its strategic oil reserves are among the world's largest and stood at more than 400 million barrels as of December.
IEA members agreed on March 11 to tap oil stockpiles to cushion the surge in prices caused by the war in the Middle East –by far the largest-ever response of its kind.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Gerakan acts as moderating force in PN, says Lau
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Gerakan can serve as a moderating force within Perikatan Nasional (PN) to address issues affecting non-Muslims, its president Datuk Dr Dominic Lau said.
He said while others were quick to condemn the Terengganu authorities over a directive requiring karaoke centres to close at 10pm, Gerakan had engaged with Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar to resolve the matter.
"If you choose the easy route, you attack Dr Sam (Samsuri), linking Perikatan Nasional to non-Malays and non-Muslims.
"But we resolved it.
"We held discussions with the exco, but it could not be settled. We then engaged with the Menteri Besar.
"So we used our own approach, an unconventional one, to solve the problem," he said in his speech at the party's 58th anniversary celebration here today.
Lau said Gerakan, as a multi-ethnic party, has the potential to gain broad appeal among Malaysians.
He also urged the party to mobilise its machinery at all levels, as he expects the 16th General Election to be held this year.
"Melaka and Johor are already before us — elections there could happen at any time.
"We expect the general election to be held this year.
"I want all centres within Parti Gerakan Rakyat Malaysia, from branches and divisions to the state and central levels, right down to the grassroots, to start now," he said.
He urged party members to reach out to the people and listen to their voices.
"Show them that Gerakan has the strength and capacity to shape Malaysia's future," he added.
Meanwhile, component parties within PN are currently negotiating seat allocations for the state elections in Melaka and Johor, as well as the general election, although no decision has been finalised.
Lau said the party election has been postponed to ensure unity ahead of the looming general election.
He added that the postponement is lawful under the party constitution, with elections to be held eight months after the general election.
He also reminded members to prioritise the party's interests over personal gain.
"There may be a small number of individuals, including some leaders, who have personal agendas.
"They may dislike certain leaders or feel dissatisfied because the party does not follow their preferred approach.
"Some may be unhappy because they did not receive any positions.
"As a result, they express dissatisfaction and make baseless criticisms that are not beneficial to the party," he said.
Lau reminded members that the party is governed by its rules and constitution.
He said Gerakan could help shape the nation if it secures 40 parliamentary seats, citing its track record of resolving issues rather than merely criticising policies.
"If you give us 40 members of parliament like DAP, we can change Malaysia," he said.
Lau added that Gerakan is studying trends to adapt to the changing political landscape, noting that he expects a hung parliament in the next election.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Yinson shares up despite weaker FY26 net profit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Yinson Holdings Bhd shares were up in early trading despite the company's net profit declining to RM683 million for the financial year ended Jan 31, 2026 (FY26) from RM1.24 billion a year ago.
At 10.44am, Yinson stock rose four sen to RM2.33 with 2.26 million shares traded.
In a research note today, Kenanga Investment Bank Bhd said Yinson's FY26 results came slightly above expectations due to lower-than-expected green energy losses.
"We trimmed FY27 forecast earnings slightly after bookkeeping adjustments for the full year. As a result, our target price was also trimmed to RM2.76. We maintain an "Outperform" call on the stock," it said.
Kenanga Investment Bank said the company is trading at a reasonable FY27 forecast price-to-earnings ratio of 10.3 times, given its potential to monetise its FPSO assets in the near to medium term.
Meanwhile, RHB Investment Bank Bhd said Yinson's medium-term outlook is underpinned by a growing base of recurring FPSO charter income and a strong US$19.5 billion backlog, which provides long-term earnings visibility.
"While reported earnings may remain volatile due to foreign exchange and mark-to-market movements, underlying profitability is expected to strengthen further with full-year contributions from FY27 onward.
RHB Investment Bank has a "Buy" call with a target price of RM3.83 per share.
In a Bursa Malaysia filing last Thursday, the logistics company said the decrease in Yinson's FY26 net profit was mainly due to lower contribution from engineering, procurement, construction, installation, and commissioning (EPCIC) activities, and higher administrative expenses following its transition from a capital expenditure-intensive EPCIC phase to an operational phase.
Impairment losses for its renewables and green technologies segments also affected profit. The company also said a one-off gain was recorded in the fourth quarter of FY25.
Revenue fell to RM5.44 billion in FY26 from RM7.60 billion in FY25.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Explainer: Why flights are getting costlier
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Jet fuel remains one of the most significant cost components for airlines, typically accounting for 20 to 25 per cent of total operating expenses.
Prior to the recent geopolitical escalation in the Middle East, prices were relatively stable at around US$85 to US$90 per barrel. However, the sharp surge to between US$150 and US$200 per barrel has dramatically changed the cost landscape, placing intense strain on airline finances globally.
The repercussions are quickly felt by passengers. Higher airfares, the reintroduction or increase of fuel surcharges, and potential flight disruptions are becoming increasingly common, underscoring how geopolitical tensions can cascade through the global travel ecosystem.
Industry experts say what begins as a conflict in a distant region ultimately translates into tangible costs for travellers, illustrating the direct link between energy markets and aviation.
The pressure is particularly acute given the industry's traditionally thin profit margins, which often range between 3.0 and 5.0 per cent annually. This leaves airlines highly vulnerable to sudden cost shocks. In some cases, carriers have indicated that at current fuel price levels, fuel expenses alone could far exceed profits recorded even in their strongest financial years, highlighting the severity of the situation, they said.
Beyond costs, operational challenges are also mounting. Airspace restrictions and safety concerns have forced airlines to suspend or reroute certain flights, making some routes less viable or profitable. This has compelled carriers to remain agile, redeploying aircraft and adjusting network strategies to focus on more resilient markets.
To manage these pressures, airlines are increasingly relying on fare adjustments as a critical risk-mitigation tool. While some have benefited from fuel hedging strategies that temporarily cushion the impact of price spikes, others – particularly those without sufficient hedging coverage – are being forced to respond more immediately.
In practice, these adjustments take two primary forms: the imposition or increase of fuel surcharges layered onto ticket prices and upward revisions to base fares to offset broader operating cost increases. Without such measures, airlines risk significant financial losses and potential disruptions to their operations.
According to Reuters, carriers across the globe are now moving swiftly to recalibrate—raising ticket prices, reassessing route networks, and revising financial projections. From Europe to Asia, the industry's response reflects a delicate balance between caution and urgency as airlines navigate an increasingly volatile operating environment.
In Greece, Aegean Airlines warned that suspended Middle East flights and soaring fuel costs would "notably impact" its first-quarter results.
Meanwhile, Air France-KLM announced plans to raise long-haul fares by 50 euros (US$57) per round trip, hoping to cushion the blow of higher fuel bills.
Down under, Air New Zealand led early fare adjustments, introducing increases of NZ$10 (US$6) on domestic flights, NZ$20 for short-haul international journeys, and NZ$90 on long-haul routes. The carrier also suspended its 2026 earnings forecast amid unprecedented market volatility.
Similarly, Qantas Airways revealed international fare hikes and is considering expanding capacity on European routes.
In Asia, fuel surcharges have become widespread.
India's Akasa Air introduced a surcharge of 199–1,300 rupees (US$2–US$14), while IndiGo levied charges of 900 rupees for Middle East flights and 2,300 rupees for Europe.
Cathay Pacific doubled surcharges on all routes, and Hong Kong Airlines hiked charges up to 35.2 per cent, with steep increases for flights to the Maldives, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Vietnam Airlines has petitioned its government to remove the environmental tax on jet fuel, facing a 70 per cent jump in operating costs.
US carriers are also affected.
American Airlines anticipates US$400 million in extra first-quarter expenses, and United Airlines plans to cut unprofitable flights while bracing for fuel costs above US$100 per barrel until 2027. Even Frontier Airlines is reviewing its full-year forecast in response to the price surge.
In Southeast Asia, Cebu Air is keeping a close eye on pricing strategies, while Thai Airways has announced fare increases of 10–15 per cent.
Australia's Virgin Australia is following suit, adjusting fares to account for rising costs linked to Middle East tensions.
Meanwhile, British Airways' parent company, IAG, has opted for patience, relying on fuel hedges to delay any immediate price hikes.
And across Europe, SAS has been forced to cancel 1,000 flights in April, citing the impossibility of absorbing skyrocketing fuel costs despite prior fare increases.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Smooth traffic on major highways, surge expected from noon
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Traffic on main highways heading towards the Klang Valley remains smooth and under control this morning.
A Malaysian Highway Authority (LLM) spokesperson said traffic is expected to increase from noon until late evening as travellers return home following the Aidilfitri holidays.
PLUS has activated the Smart Lane at 34 locations from 9am to 10pm today to ensure smoother traffic flow, he said.
Highway users are encouraged to plan their journeys based on travel time recommendations provided by concessionaires and to ensure they are well-rested before driving.
Real-time traffic updates are available through the LLM toll-free hotline at 1-800-88-7752 or via live footage on the agency's official website.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two killed in car-motorcycle collision
Article:
IPOH: Two men were killed in an accident involving a car and a motorcycle at Kampung Seri Makmur, Batu 7, near Batu Kurau early this morning.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said the department received an emergency call at 1.24am.
A team from the Batu Kurau station was immediately dispatched to the scene.
Upon arrival, rescuers found a 26-year-old passenger pinned beneath the Perodua Bezza, while the 21-year-old motorcyclist had been thrown from his Yamaha Ego Gear. Both were confirmed dead at the scene, he said in a statement.
The driver of the car, a man in his 20s, sustained injuries and was rescued by bystanders before being transported to Parit Buntar Hospital.
The bodies were handed over to the police for further investigation. The operation ended at 2.50am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 50 urns stolen in a month: What we know so far
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: More than 50 burial urns have been stolen from three columbaria across three memorial parks in a month, prompting a federal investigation into the thefts.
The thefts first came to light on March 9, when more than 20 urns were
reported missing
from the Perpetual Memorial Park in Kulai, Johor.
Kulai police chief Assistant Commissioner Tan Seng Lee confirmed the incident and said the suspects have yet to be identified.
The memorial park management later clarified that 22 urns were stolen from a specific section and that families of the deceased had been notified. It added that it was working closely with the police.
On March 18, Xiao En Memorial Park revealed it was also a victim, with more than
30 urns stolen
from its Nilai site in late February.
Nilai police chief Superintendent Johari Yahya said the Xiao En management had received a ransom demand from an international number via WhatsApp. The caller claimed to be in possession of the missing urns.
Xiao En said it was conducting an internal investigation while assisting police.
The following day, Nirvana Asia Group released a statement saying its facilities
were also affected
.
While the company did not disclose the location, timing, or number of urns involved, it said the items had been retrieved.
Nirvana said it suspected Cambodian scam syndicates were behind the thefts affecting various columbaria, noting the incidents did not appear random or isolated.
The company said the thieves showed signs of premeditated and organised criminal activity.
Following the series of thefts, Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail confirmed the Federal Criminal Investigation Department (CID) has been instructed to investigate the matter.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bahrain, a kingdom deeply proud of its seafaring heritage
Article:
MANAMA (Bahrain): "Did you see the bubbles?"
Abbas Al Faraj, a licensed tour guide in Bahrain, pointed towards the shallow waters near Bahrain Fort (Qal'at al-Bahrain) on the northern coast of the island, where steady bursts of water rise quietly from beneath the seabed.
"Do you know what that is?" he asked.
"Water bubbles," I replied, without thinking much of it.
"He smiled. "That is Bahrain," he said.
Origins of 'Two Seas'
The bubbles, he explained, mark the meeting of underground freshwater springs and the saltwater of the Arabian Gulf, a rare natural phenomenon that gave the kingdom its name, derived from the Arabic phrase Al-Baḥrayn, which means "two seas".
The Kingdom of Bahrain, an archipelago of 33 natural and man-made islands, is located in the Arabian Gulf and has a population of about 1.5 million.
Long before oil wealth and international sporting events, this "meeting of waters" sustained early settlements and enabled Bahrain to flourish as a maritime trading hub linking Mesopotamia, the Indus Valley and beyond.
Standing there in the cool February breeze, with the Gulf wind cutting gently across the fort grounds, it became clear to me that Bahrain's story is not confined to a single monument.
(The writer was in Bahrain on a familiarisation trip organised by the Bahrain Tourism and Exhibitions Authority in mid-February 2026.)
At Qal'at al-Bahrain, a UNESCO World Heritage Site that was occupied by humans for nearly 4,500 years, the scale of preservation is hard to miss.
The archaeological grounds stretch openly along the coastline, largely free of commercial intrusion.
Entry to the fort is free, with only a modest fee charged for the adjacent museum.
There are no long rows of souvenir stalls lining the pathways. Visitors move through layers of history with space to pause and absorb. It feels deliberate.
Abbas, dressed in Bahrain's traditional white jubah and headscarf, conducted the tour in fluent English.
A native Arabic speaker who is also conversant in Spanish, he spoke with quiet pride. He did not rush. He did not sell. He explained.
"Our history is rooted in trade," he said.
After leaving the fort, our group moved on to the next stop. Along the way, I noticed replicas of traditional wooden maritime vessels across the island, along waterfronts, near roundabouts and even inside hotel corridors where our group stayed, reminders of Bahrain's seafaring past.
A modern nation, yet deeply proud of its seafaring identity, I thought.
The pearling path and maritime legacy
From the fort's open grounds overlooking the Gulf, we made our way into narrower streets and older neighbourhoods.
In Muharraq, the 3.5-kilometre Pearling Path links restored merchant houses, former oyster beds and coastal fortifications that once formed the backbone of the island's economy.
For centuries, pearl divers plunged repeatedly into Gulf waters, holding their breath for one to two minutes at a time and making dozens of descents in a single day.
It was exhausting and dangerous work that demanded endurance and skill.
"I am a diver too," Abbas said with a laugh, before adding that he could manage about a minute underwater.
"But not like my ancestors. They could dive much longer."
Much of Pearling Path can be explored freely.
The emphasis is on interpretation and continuity rather than merchandise.
The restored houses, traditional diving equipment and preserved streetscape allow visitors to see how the pearl industry once flourished there.
Souq Al Manama
Later, our group went to Souq Al Manama, where history shifts from curated preservation to living trade. Its narrow lanes are lined with spice, textile and gold shops.
Traders greet visitors politely, keeping a respectful distance.
The atmosphere feels familiar, reminiscent of Masjid India in Kuala Lumpur, where traditional commerce adapts quietly within a modern city.
Afterwards, we had lunch at a local restaurant that offered another glimpse into the daily rhythm.
Meals were served communally on tables covered with simple plastic sheets — practical, hygienic and generous.
Before leaving the souq, I bought a kilogramme of chocolate candies as a small memento for colleagues and family back home.
Tourism and global engagement
For me, Bahrain is not frozen in the past. The kingdom hosts the Formula One Bahrain Grand Prix and continues to attract international visitors, with tourism contributing to around seven percent of its gross domestic product.
It is modern, connected and outward-looking.
Yet what stayed with me was neither Bahrain's race circuit nor the city skyline.
It was when Abbas pointed to the rising bubbles and said, "That is Bahrain".
Perhaps what he meant was this: Bahrain is built not only on what it once drew from the sea, such as trade, pearls and passage, but also on what it chose to preserve.
Profit has its place, yet identity often outlives commerce.
Malaysia, too, was born of the waves.
Our history once centred on the Strait of Malacca, an empire defined by a seafaring identity every bit as deep as Bahrain's.
If we can reclaim that same pride in our origins, perhaps we too can one day point to our own horizons and say, with equal conviction: "That is Malaysia". — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE AM MAR 24, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened higher on Tuesday, tracking overnight gains on Wall Street after US President Donald Trump extended the deadline for possible strikes on Iranian infrastructure.
Crude oil prices also declined, with Brent crude falling below US$100 per barrel.
The local bourse remains well supported and is expected to trade within the 1,715 to 1,730 range today.
In the crypto market, Bitcoin rose to RM278,967.
Ethereum climbed to RM8,449, while Solana eased to RM358.
That's it for Market Pulse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Disturbing footage of indecent act in Penang cemetery sparks police probe, two now in custody
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, March 24 — Police have arrested two individuals suspected of engaging in an indecent act at a cemetery in Batu Gantung, Air Itam, after a video of the incident went viral on social media.
Timur Laut police chief ACP Abdul Rozak Muhammad said a report was received from a 69-year-old man about the video, which was recorded at a Chinese cemetery.
Preliminary investigations indicate the incident occurred on March 22, and both suspects are believed to be locals.
“The first suspect, a 37-year-old woman, was arrested yesterday around 8.35pm at a flat near Jalan Padang Tembak.
“The second suspect, a 58-year-old man, was arrested later the same night at 11.10pm in front of a building near Jalan Paya Terubong, Bayan Lepas,” he said in a statement today.
Abdul Rozak added that the suspects have admitted their involvement and have been remanded for three days, until March 26, to assist in investigations under Section 294(a) and Section 509 of the Penal Code, as well as Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998.
The 38-second video shows the naked couple in the cemetery, recorded by a woman who confronted them, warning that she would report the matter to the police. — Bernama

Title: Elderly mum and son found dead in Sg Petani home after more than two days
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI, March 24 — An elderly woman and her son were found dead in their home in Kampung Zainol Abidin, Bedong, yesterday evening.
Kuala Muda police chief ACP Hanyan Ramlan said the victims were a man in his 40s and his mother in her 70s.
“A report was received at 6.05pm yesterday. Initial inspection found no injuries caused by sharp weapons. As the house was locked from the inside, we had to break in. The bodies were found in the living room and the kitchen,” he said in a statement today.
According to him, further investigation is still ongoing, and a post-mortem examination is expected to be conducted this morning.
“The victims are believed to have died more than 48 hours earlier, and the case is currently classified as a sudden death report (SDR) pending post-mortem results,” he said.
Meanwhile, in SHAH ALAM, Selangor,a lorry driver died after the vehicle he was driving skidded off the road and overturned into a drain in an accident at Simpang Jalan Suara, Kampung Tambak Jawa, Kuala Selangor, yesterday.
Thevictim, identified as Taqiuddin Jamil, 38, was reported to have been trapped in the driver’s seat after the three-ton lorry he was driving lost control.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) assistant director Ahmad Mukhlis Mokhtar said the department received a call about the incident at 6.32am, and a team from the Kuala Selangor Fire and Rescue Station rushed to the scene.
“Upon arriving at the scene at 6.54am, firefighters found a male victim trapped in the driver’s seat and used rescue cutter equipment to extricate him.
“The victim was successfully removed at 8.50am before being confirmed dead by Ministry of Health personnel at the scene. The body was later handed over to the police for further action,” he said in a statement today. — Bernama

Title: Johor petrol station under probe after viral videos of subsidised RON95 poured into car boot container
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, March 24 — The Johor Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) is investigating a petrol station in Mutiara Rini here that allegedly allowed two people to fill a container with subsidised RON95 petrol instead of directly into their vehicle tank.
Its director Lilis Saslinda Pornomo said investigators will be looking into the petrol station’s closed-circuit television recordings, as well as examining transaction records and purchase receipts for verification purposes.
“We found two transactions involving RON95 petrol purchases, with one into the vehicle’s tank, while the other was into a plastic container in the vehicle’s boot with a quantity that exceeded the limit allowed by the Malaysian Supply Controller.
“During the inspection, the KPDN enforcement team issued a warning to the petrol station’s management on the importance of complying with regulations and increasing their monitoring to prevent such incidents from recurring.
“Johor KPDN will also undertake follow-up action by tracking down and calling in the vehicle owner for further investigations,” he said in a Facebook statement.
Lilis Saslinda said the case is being investigated under Subregulation 12A of the Control of Supplies Regulations 1974 for suspected violation of the instructions of the Malaysian Controller of Supplies.
Penalties include a maximum fine of RM1 million, jail up to three years, or both for those found guilty.
For subsequent offences, the fine can be raised up to RM3 million, jail up to five years, or both.
Videos have been shared on social media showing two people filling a container inside in a car boot; the vehicle had Johor registration plates.
A video also showed the pump price of RM230 for more than 71 litres of subsidised RON95.
Buying subsidised petrol or diesel using containers or tanks without a permit is against the law.
The Finance Ministry has said it will block the cards of individuals found to have abused the Budi Madani RON95 assistance programme.

Title: Your shopping bill is about to get more expensive: Farmers say veggie prices could rise 50pc in April
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 24 — Malaysians should brace themselves for an increase in vegetable prices as early as next week as the effects of the West Asia fights make its way to our shores.
Cameron Highlands Malay Farmers Association chairman Datuk Syed Abd Rahman Syed Abd Rashid said the hike could happen within the next week or two,
New Strait Times
reported yesterday.
“Prices may surge by up to 50 per cent for a short period before potentially stabilising at a 30 per cent increase,” he was quoted as saying.
He attributed the increase to rising fertiliser and diesel costs, which are essential for machinery, generators, and transport.
Armed conflicts in the Gulf region, particularly the Strait of Hormuz – a crucial sea passage for the world’s fuel and trade goods – are pushing up global oil prices, which increase fuel and fertiliser costs, ultimately making everyday goods like vegetables more expensive.
Malaysia Muslim Consumers Association chief activist Nadzim Johan advised consumers to spend wisely amid the looming price hikes.
He encouraged households to start home gardening using hydroponics for vegetables such as spinach, water spinach, and mustard greens.
“Consumers have the power to influence prices. By making informed choices and comparing prices, traders will have no choice but to lower them,” he told
New Strait Times
.
He added that schools, clubs, and associations could adopt similar small-scale hydroponic projects to benefit students and members.
The rising cost of raw materials is likely to be felt by the end consumer. — Picture by Lee Khang Yi
The extreme heat on the local front is also affecting domestic agriculture, with rice fields outside the Muda Agricultural Development Authority areas in Kedah – Malaysia’s paddy production hub – reporting poor yields due to limited irrigation.
MetMalaysia has forecasted heatwaves, with temperatures between 37° and 40°Celsius daily in certain areas, notably the northern peninsula.
Yesterday,
Harian Metro
reported farmers, fishermen and residents in parts of Kedah and Perlis saying their rivers and other water supply sources were shrinking, and expressed concern about their dwindling yield.

Title: Education Ministry expands School in Hospital programme to Penang, helping students stay on track
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 24 — The Ministry of Education (MOE) is continuously striving to meet the needs of every segment of students, including those undergoing treatment in hospitals, says Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek.
She said that to ensure children receiving treatment did not fall behind in their studies, the MOE was collaborating with the Ministry of Health (MOH) and the Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE) to implement the School in Hospital (SDH) programme.
According to Fadhlina, there were currently 20 SDH programmes operating nationwide, with more than 400,000 students having benefited since the initiative’s inception.
“Most recently, the SDH programme has been expanded to the Seberang Jaya Hospital, making it the first to be implemented in Penang,” she said in a Facebook post yesterday.
Fadhlina said the continuous efforts were also reflected in the Malaysia Education Plan (RPM) 2026-2035, which emphasised inclusive education and equitable access.
The SDH programme has been implemented since 2011. — Bernama

Title: Pop-up fireworks stall in JB goes up in flames, damages nearby Proton Gen-2 and food truck
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, March 24 —  A stall selling firecrackers and fireworks was destroyed in a fire here last night.
No casualties were reported in the incident.
Larkin Fire and Rescue Station (BBP) operations commander, Senior Fire Officer I Nakish Nekmat said 11 personnel were rushed to the fire at Majidee Corner 1C, Jalan Bunga Ros, Kampung Dato Sulaiman Menteri, after receiving an emergency call at 8.32 pm.
He said the blaze also involved two vehicles, a Proton Gen-2, which was 80 per cent destroyed, and a food truck, which sustained 73 per cent damage.
“The pop-up tent used for selling the firecrackers and fireworks was 90 per cent destroyed,” he said in a statement, adding that the fire was brought under control at 8.42 pm, and mop-up operations are currently ongoing.
Earlier, a 44-second video clip of the fire involving the stall had gone viral on social media. — Bernama

Title: Mum and two children dead, two others hurt in Perodua Aruz skid crash near Dungun
Article:
DUNGUN, March 24 — A mother and her two children were killed after the sport utility vehicle (SUV) they were travelling in skidded at Kilometre 59 of Jalan Jerangau-Jabor, near Jerangau Felda here, yesterday.
In the 6.10 pm incident, two of them died at the scene, while the other died at the Jerangau Health Clinic.
It is learnt that two other children, who were also in the SUV, sustained injuries and were taken to Dungun Hospital.
However, all the victims have yet to be identified.
Dungun police chief Supt Maizura Abdul Kadir, who confirmed the incident, said further details will follow.
Meanwhile, when contacted by Bernama, Al-Muktafi Billah Shah (AMBS) Fire and Rescue Station head Mohd Izzuddin Mahmood said it dispatched a team to the scene after receiving an emergency call about the accident.
He said that, upon arrival, the team found that a Perodua Aruz had crashed into an electric pole at the roadside.
He added that there were five people in the SUV, with three of them extricated by firefighters using special equipment.
“Two others were rescued earlier by members of the public,” he added. — Bernama

Title: Abuse Budi95, lose it: Finance Ministry to freeze cards over subsidised fuel misuse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 24 — The Ministry of Finance (MOF) will block the cards of individuals found to have abused the Budi Madani RON95 (Budi95) assistance programme by filling subsidised RON95 petrol into empty barrels or containers, as has gone viral recently.
MOF stressed that such action clearly violates regulations because it constitutes misuse of fuel subsidies under the Budi95 initiative and could affect the government’s efforts to ensure transparent, targeted and effective subsidy management.
“Investigations found that the individual involved is a Malaysian and a MyKad holder. Following that, the MOF will take action by blocking the identity card used during that transaction, as well as the vehicle owner, from continuing to enjoy Budi95,” it said in a statement today.
